danger_signals.visual_form: Large sprite animations of theropods and sauropods
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player character; encounter initiates combat or chase sequence

danger_signals.visual_form: HUD element showing player vitality depletion
danger_signals.meaning: Player character sustaining damage; death imminent if depleted

danger_signals.visual_form: Broken facility structures, collapsed barriers, overgrown vegetation reclaiming park infrastructure
danger_signals.meaning: Park containment systems failing; danger zone expanding

danger_signals.visual_form: Audio-visual event with screen shake and creature silhouettes

safety_signals.visual_form: Indoor rooms with walls, doors, technical equipment visible
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary shelter from open-air dinosaur encounters; enclosed safe zone

safety_signals.visual_form: Pickup sprites: bullets, first-aid kits scattered in environment
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability; player empowerment and survival capacity restored

safety_signals.visual_form: Fences, gates, reinforced walls in functional state
safety_signals.meaning: Containment active; designated safe pathways through zones

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Facility logos on buildings and signage; technical infrastructure aesthetic
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Functional facilities, control rooms, administrative structures with maintained appearance (initially); signs of corporate order

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Creature designs (realistic animalistic forms); natural textures and overgrowth
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different dinosaur species represent different threat tiers and behaviors
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Overgrown areas, broken containment, organic decay of facility; zones progressively consumed by nature

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Facility structures intact, organized layout, maintained pathways, controlled lighting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dinosaurs contained behind barriers; limited free roaming; structured enemy encounters

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visible damage to barriers, broken fences, power outages affecting lighting, structural deterioration
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dinosaurs roaming freely; increased encounter frequency; predators appear in previously safe zones

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Extensive structural damage, overgrowth dominating environment, minimal functional infrastructure, darkness/decay aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dinosaur encounters unavoidable; ecosystem dominates park space; human control absent

environmental_storytelling.example: Player moves from organized administrative areas through maintenance sections to outdoor paddocks, each visually showing increasing entropy and nature reclamation
Intact vs. breached fences, working vs. failed gates, and powered vs. unpowered barriers visually communicate the cascade of system failures
Scattered equipment, signage, and facility remnants indicate human presence and attempted control prior to abandonment
Creature presence in specific zones (paddocks, labs) indicates reclaimed territory and ecosystem reformation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Normal movement speed; weapon ready; active exploration posture
Reduced movement speed; visual limp or damage indicator; cautious behavior
Aggressive charge animation; roar/vocalization; pursuit of player; destructive interactions with environment
Slower movement; cyclical patrol path; less immediate threat response
Opens on approach; functional animation; access granted
Jammed or forced open; remains in open state; visual damage

notes: Jurassic Park Genesis (1993) uses visual hierarchy to communicate world-state: intact facility = containment/safety, degraded facility = breach/danger. Dinosaur encounter design relies on sprite-based threat communication through size, animation intensity, and positioning. The game's progression implicitly communicates political/system failure through environmental visual decay—a visual manifestation of corporate control loss. Faction identity is primarily environmental rather than explicit (no color-coded enemies or uniforms); InGen presence communicated through infrastructure, dinosaur presence through creature design and behavioral animations.
sources_used: Wikipedia - Jurassic Park (disambiguation) [confirms game exists as 1993 Genesis title; limited technical detail available]
